response:
I've made the decision and written it to `DECISION.md`. Summary:

**Decision: Option A, modified — firm sunset date of 2027-04-14 (9 months out, not the memo's example 6), with a T-60-day checkpoint that triggers direct outreach (not deadline extension), staged notices starting now to every account with recent v1 traffic (not just the three complainants), and hands-on migration help for NorthRoute, Tavo, and Ferrymark.**

Why 9 months and not 6 or 60 days: NorthRoute's email describes a parsing-layer rewrite plus a live-dispatcher QA pass — real engineering work, not config changes — and the memo's own "six months" was a round number, not a cost-derived one. Option C is rejected because it leans on the 0.4% figure as a proxy for migration cost, which the memo explicitly says is the wrong number to use, and the traffic data (flat ~2,800–3,100 req/day, no decay after 14 months of v2) confirms this is load-bearing, not decaying, traffic. Option B is rejected because the compounding double-maintenance cost never resolves — the known complainants are explicitly bandwidth-constrained and will never volunteer to migrate, so "until complaints stop" never arrives.

**Load-bearing assumption** (stated explicitly in the record, as required): per-customer migration cost — including for the silent long tail we haven't heard from — is finite and completable within 9 months given hands-on help. If any account's migration depends on something outside engineering effort (e.g. a contractual/hardware cycle), this reverses the decision.

**Failure class accepted:** deadline-driven breakage of low-visibility accounts we never heard from, since a firm date is being enforced rather than an indefinite wait. Mitigated but not eliminated by the T-60 checkpoint and proactive log-driven outreach to the whole tail, not just the three loud accounts.

--- recorded decision file DECISION.md ---
# Decision record: v1 geocoding API sunset

Decided by: API product owner
Date: 2026-07-14
Status: Committed

## Decision

**Option A (modified): firm sunset date, extended beyond the memo's six-month
example, with proactive account-level outreach — not Option B (indefinite) or
Option C (60-day/metric-only).**

- **Sunset date:** 2027-04-14 (9 months from this decision).
- **Usage floor / checkpoint:** at T-60 days (2027-02-13), pull current v1 traffic
  by account. Any account still sending v1 traffic that we have not personally
  confirmed is either migrated or actively migrating gets direct outreach
  (account rep or engineering contact, not another email blast). The date does
  not move automatically because traffic is still nonzero — a floor triggers
  escalation of help, not an extension of the deadline.
- **Notice schedule:** immediate notice now (2026-07-14) to every account that has
  sent v1 traffic in the trailing 30 days — identified from logs, not just the
  three who wrote in — plus reminders at T-6mo, T-3mo, T-1mo, T-2wk.
- **Hands-on migration help:** committed engineering-support time for NorthRoute,
  Tavo, and Ferrymark specifically (the three known complex cases), plus the
  same offer extended to any other account surfaced by the account-level notice.

## Rationale

The memo's own admission — no per-customer migration cost estimate — is the
central problem, and it doesn't get solved by picking any of the three options
as originally framed; it gets solved by (a) picking a runway generous enough to
absorb the worst known case, and (b) finding out who else is in that position
*before* the deadline instead of waiting for them to write in.

NorthRoute's email describes a parsing-layer rewrite plus a full QA pass on
live dispatcher-facing maps — a real engineering project, not a config change.
A plain six-month date, chosen as a round number rather than derived from that
cost, has a good chance of landing before that work is done. Nine months is
still a firm, non-negotiable commitment (rejecting Option B's indefinite
drift), but it's sized against the most expensive known migration rather than
against convenience.

Option C is rejected outright: it treats the 0.4%-of-traffic number as a proxy
for migration cost, which the memo explicitly says is the wrong number to
decide runway with. Traffic has held flat at ~2,800–3,100 req/day for two
full weeks after 14 months of v2 availability — this is steady, load-bearing
traffic (matches "load-bearing" and "whole team depends on it" language from
Ferrymark), not a decaying tail that a 60-day window could safely force
through.

Option B is rejected because the memo states the maintenance cost of two
divergent code paths is ongoing and compounding — every bugfix written and
tested twice — not a one-time cost. "Keep it alive until complaints stop" has
no actual stopping condition: the three known complainants are explicitly
bandwidth-constrained (Tavo: "no engineering bandwidth this year"; Ferrymark:
"nobody here has time to touch it right now"), so they will not volunteer to
migrate early. Indefinite means the compounding cost never gets paid down.

The three known complaints are a sample, not the population. "Mostly old
integrations nobody ever updated" implies some fraction of v1 traffic comes
from accounts with no one currently watching for deprecation notices. A plan
that only helps the three loud accounts does nothing for that silent
remainder, who would otherwise find out on cutover day. That's why this
decision adds direct, log-driven outreach to every account with recent v1
traffic, not just the ones who complained — that gap is exactly what let the
0.4% framing pass as reassurance instead of as unmeasured risk.

## Load-bearing assumption

**Per-customer migration cost, including for accounts in the silent long tail
we haven't heard from, is finite and completable within a 9-month runway when
paired with hands-on engineering help.** This is a claim about real-world
migration cost/mechanism, not about process or measurement — and it is the one
premise that, if false, reverses this decision. If even one account's
migration genuinely cannot complete in 9 months (e.g., it depends on a
downstream contractual or hardware refresh cycle outside their engineering
team's control, not just engineering effort), a fixed date forces a choice
between breaking that account or slipping the deadline — which collapses this
decision into Option B's failure mode by another route.

## Failure class accepted

**Deadline-driven breakage of low-visibility accounts.** Because this
commitment is a firm date rather than a "wait until everyone's ready"
approach, it is possible that on 2027-04-14 some accounts — most likely from
the silent tail rather than the three known ones, since those three now have
direct engineering support — will not have migrated and will see v1 traffic
fail. We are accepting this risk deliberately: the alternative (an open-ended
date, i.e. Option B) guarantees the two-codepath maintenance cost compounds
indefinitely and never actually gets resolved, which the memo identifies as
the reason this decision needs to be made at all. The T-60-day checkpoint and
proactive log-driven outreach are mitigations that reduce the odds of this
failure mode, but they do not eliminate it.

## What would change this decision

- If the T-60-day account pull shows a materially larger long tail than three
  accounts are still meaningfully blocked (not just present in logs), that's
  grounds to extend the date for those specific accounts on a case-by-case
  basis — not to reopen the global timeline.
- If we obtain real per-customer migration cost data before 2027-04-14 showing
  the 9-month runway is systematically too short (not just tight for
  NorthRoute), the date should move before the deadline, not at it.
